nativeRange.habitat: The Pacu inhabits large river floodplains and flooded forests known as várzea across the Amazon and Orinoco basins. During the annual flood season it moves into inundated forest, where it feeds on fruits and seeds that fall into the water. It tolerates a wide range of water conditions, including oxygen-poor blackwater rivers and sediment-rich whitewater rivers.

decorPreferences: large smooth boulders or driftwood (if kept at all)

diet.notes: The Pacu is a herbivore-leaning omnivore whose wild diet is dominated by the fruits, seeds, and nuts that fall into flooded forests — a feeding strategy known as frugivory. It also eats aquatic plants, algae, invertebrates, and occasionally small fish. Its powerful, molar-like teeth evolved specifically for crushing hard seeds and nuts, and are notably different in shape from the pointed teeth of its piranha relatives. In captivity it accepts large pellets, cichlid sticks, cut fruit such as banana, melon, and grapes, leafy vegetables, and earthworms. Feeding once or twice daily is recommended; large adults produce a very high biological load on filtration systems.

goodWith: similarly-sized robust fish in public-aquarium settings
large catfish (Pseudoplatystoma, large Pimelodidae) in public-aquarium setups
avoidWith: small fish (may be consumed incidentally despite herbivorous diet)
all home aquarium tankmates (wholly inappropriate for home aquaria)
piranha relatives where aggression risk exists for juvenile pacu

imageCandidates.notes: Adult specimen showing body profile and coloration. FishBase image ID Comac_u3.
Large adult in aquarium; clear body and fin detail.

summary: The Pacu (Colossoma macropomum), also known as Tambaqui, is one of the largest freshwater fish in South America, capable of reaching over a metre in length and 30 kg. A member of the piranha family, it is generally considered peaceful and uses blunt, molar-like teeth to crush seeds and fruit. Adult size makes it suitable only for public aquarium facilities.
careNotes:
The Pacu is the largest member of the piranha family (Serrasalmidae) and one of the largest freshwater fish in South America, routinely exceeding 80 cm and 20 kg in the wild, with documented specimens reaching 108 cm and over 30 kg. It is among the most commercially important food fish in Amazonian aquaculture, farmed throughout tropical South America. Despite sharing a family with piranhas, the Pacu is generally considered peaceful and is built very differently: its teeth are blunt and molar-like, evolved for crushing hard fruits, seeds, and nuts rather than for biting prey. The resemblance of its dentition to human molars has made it the subject of widely-shared photographs.

Pacu are frequently sold in the ornamental trade as juveniles of 5–10 cm, where they are sometimes mislabeled or presented without adequate information about adult size. Within one to two years they outgrow virtually any home aquarium, creating serious welfare and practical problems for unprepared keepers. Overwhelmed hobbyists have released specimens into the wild in many parts of the world; individual fish have been caught across a large number of US states and in various non-native regions, though self-sustaining wild populations have not been confirmed outside their native range.

Adult Pacu require open water at enormous volume — a minimum of 10,000 litres with a tank length of at least 600 cm is considered the baseline for responsible husbandry — along with robust industrial-grade filtration to handle the species’ very high biological waste output. Preferred water temperature is 23–30°C, with soft to moderately hard, slightly acidic to neutral water (pH 5.5–7.5) reflecting Amazonian conditions. Only public aquariums or permitted aquaculture facilities realistically have the infrastructure to house adult specimens.
breedingNotes: The Pacu cannot be bred in a home aquarium setting, and captive reproduction in any private facility is not considered feasible. In the wild, spawning takes place during the Amazon flood season as fish migrate into flooded forest areas. Commercial aquaculture operations induce spawning hormonally through a technique called hypophysation, carried out in large outdoor pond facilities. Females are capable of producing hundreds of thousands of eggs per spawn. Aquaculture breeding is well-established in Brazil, Venezuela, Colombia, and across tropical Latin America, where the Pacu is farmed primarily as a food fish.

primary.name: FishBase — Colossoma macropomum
primary.notes: Primary source. Adult size (to 108 cm TL, commonly 70 cm), max weight 30 kg, temperature 23–30°C, pH range, native range (Amazon and Orinoco basins), taxonomy (Serrasalmidae, Characiformes), lifespan, diet (frugivore/omnivore), and ecology. FishBase records wild longevity up to 28 years.

additional.name: SeriouslyFish — Colossoma macropomum
additional.notes: Confirmed adult size to 108 cm, water parameters (22–30°C, pH 5.5–7.5, dH 2–15), soft to moderately hard Amazonian water, habitat (flooded forests and large river channels), frugivorous diet ecology, and public-aquarium-only suitability recommendation. Also confirms peaceful temperament and seed-crushing dentition.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Pacu Fish Care
additional.notes: Trade perspective on juvenile sale vs adult unsuitability, diet (fruits, pellets, vegetables), invasive species concern (Florida), and the human-molar teeth viral phenomenon. Confirms herbivore-leaning omnivore classification and peaceful temperament within Serrasalmidae.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Pacu species profile
additional.notes: Confirmed hobbyist advisory context: sold small, grows to public-aquarium scale. Water parameters, diet frugivory emphasis, Amazon basin range, and conservation note that wild populations are under pressure from overfishing (important food fish). Temperature 24–28°C cited.

additional.name: TFH Magazine — Giant Pacu overview
additional.notes: Background on Serrasalmidae taxonomy, comparison with piranhas, frugivory ecology including seed dispersal role in Amazonian forest, aquaculture importance in South America, and notes on invasive releases in North America and Europe. Supports expert care level and public-aquarium-only advisory.

fish.breedingNotes: Captive breeding is not achievable in any private setting. Commercial aquaculture operations induce spawning hormonally through a technique called hypophysation, carried out in large outdoor pond facilities. The Pacu is a fully domesticated aquaculture species across tropical South America, but reproduction in private aquariums is not considered feasible.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: Adults commonly exceed 80 cm and 20 kg, with documented specimens reaching 108 cm and over 30 kg. Sold as juveniles of 5–10 cm in the ornamental trade, they rapidly outgrow all home aquarium setups within one to two years. Only public aquariums or permitted aquaculture facilities can house adults responsibly.
